narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36 and plans to attend full-time, you've put together a thoughtful application for law school, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 from a lower-middle income family background. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 67% probability of failure for you, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to help ensure we select students most likely to succeed. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic performance in school, particularly your grades in later years. The most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which is well below the typical student's average of around 5.5—this low standing signaled to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands of legal studies leading up to the bar. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward passing, as it's close to the dataset average of 36.7 and shows strong reasoning ability. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed toward the failure prediction, though less strongly than Year 3, highlighting an early pattern of underperformance compared to peers who average about 5.7. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 is notably lower than the 3.21 average, which the model considered but didn't rank as the top driver here. Your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students (over 90%), so it didn't sway things much either way. Overall, the model saw your consistently low decile rankings as the biggest red flags, outweighing your decent LSAT and overriding other neutral factors like family income. While this is disappointing, it's not the end of the road—consider retaking courses or boosting your GPA through post-bac programs to improve those decile rankings, as schools often value demonstrated academic turnaround. You might also prepare more intensively for the LSAT to push it even higher, building on your already competitive score. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback on targeted improvements, and explore bridge programs or community colleges that can help rebuild your transcript before reapplying. With focused effort on these academic gaps, you can shift future predictions in your favor and pursue your legal ambitions.
